nodes.x: Now a certain man was sick, named Lazarus, of Bethany, the town of Mary and her sister Martha.
Now Jesus loved Martha, and her sister, and Lazarus.
Then Jesus six days before the passover came to Bethany, where Lazarus was which had been dead, whom he raised from the dead.
(It was that Mary which anointed the Lord with ointment, and wiped his feet with her hair, whose brother Lazarus was sick.)

It  was  natural  that  Mary,  left  now  to  herself  with  a 
secret  in  her  soul,  holier,  greater,  and  more  thrilling  than 
any  ever  borne  before  or  since,  should  seek  companionship, 
and  that  of  some  one  of  her  own  sex,  in  whom  she  could  con- 
fide, from  whom  she  might  hope  to  derive  comfort  and  sup- 
port, and  to  whom  it  would  be  not  wrong  to …
There they made him a supper; and Martha served: but Lazarus was one of them that sat at the table with him.
(It was that Mary which anointed the Lord with ointment, and wiped his feet with her hair, whose brother Lazarus was sick.)
Then took Mary a pound of ointment of spikenard, very costly, and anointed the feet of Jesus, and wiped his feet with her hair: and the house was filled with the odour of the ointment.
And she had a sister called Mary, which also sat at Jesus’ feet, and heard his word.
Now a certain man was sick, named Lazarus, of Bethany, the town of Mary and her sister Martha.
Then when Mary was come where Jesus was, and saw him, she fell down at his feet, saying unto him, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.
Therefore his sisters sent unto him, saying, Lord, behold, he whom thou lovest is sick.
And he said, O my Lord, send, I pray thee, by the hand of him whom thou wilt send.
And he sware unto her, Whatsoever thou shalt ask of me, I will give it thee, unto the half of my kingdom.
And these signs shall follow him—he shall heal the sick, he shall cast out devils, and shall be delivered from those who would administer unto him deadly poison;
Thus speaketh the Lord of hosts, saying, Execute true judgment, and shew mercy and compassions every man to his brother:

He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches— Jesus Christ. Come out, of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her „ plagues. — -A Voice from Heaven.
When Jesus heard that, he said, This sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of God, that the Son of God might be glorified thereby.
For indeed he was sick nigh unto death: but God had mercy on him; and not on him only, but on me also, lest I should have sorrow upon sorrow.

Now previous to the change into the incorruptible state which is promised 
in the resurrection of the saints, the body could be mortal (capable of 
dying) ,although not destined to die (moriturus); just as our body in its 
present state can, so to speak, be capable of sickness, although not 
destined to be sick. For whose is the flesh which is incapable of sickness, 
even if from some accident it…
Therefore his sisters sent unto him, saying, Lord, behold, he whom thou lovest is sick.
Also every sickness, and every plague, which is not written in the book of this law, them will the Lord bring upon thee, until thou be destroyed.
¶ Therefore, when he was gone out, Jesus said, Now is the Son of man glorified, and God is glorified in him.
Now Jesus loved Martha, and her sister, and Lazarus.
Now Jesus was not yet come into the town, but was in that place where Martha met him.
And Jesus answered and said unto her, Martha, Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things:
Then Martha, as soon as she heard that Jesus was coming, went and met him: but Mary sat still in the house.
When he had heard therefore that he was sick, he abode two days still in the same place where he was.

But, in the midst of his prosperity, he was called away to experience
another state of existence, with barely a moment's warning, for he was
sick only four days from the time he was first taken ill until he died,
and even on the fourth day, and in the last hour of his illness, it
was not supposed to be at all dangerous, until his son, who sat by his
bedside, discovered he was dying.

But, in the midst of his prosperity, he was called away to experience another state of existence, with barely a moment's warning, for he was sick only four days from the time he was'first taken ill until he died, and even on the fourth day, and in the last hour of his illness, it was not supposed to be at all dangerous, until his son, who sat by his bedside, discovered he was dying.
Then inquired he of them the hour when he began to amend. And they said unto him, Yesterday at the seventh hour the fever left him.
And ran through that whole region round about, and began to carry about in beds those that were sick, where they heard he was.

On his return to the settlements from the mountains, February 1st, he writes: "I have passed eleven days in the mountains in the midst of driving snow, making my bed upon the ground in a shepherd's tent. I ate my bread and meat twice a day with thanksgiving. I drank the cold snow water and indeed have had the best of health, although when I left Sunset I had a severe cold on my lungs. I also…
Then after that saith he to his disciples, Let us go into Judæa again.
His brethren therefore said unto him, Depart hence, and go into Judæa, that thy disciples also may see the works that thou doest.
Then the disciples, every man according to his ability, determined to send relief unto the brethren which dwelt in Judæa:
He left Judæa, and departed again into Galilee.
This is again the second miracle that Jesus did, when he was come out of Judæa into Galilee.
¶ After these things came Jesus and his disciples into the land of Judæa; and there he tarried with them, and baptized.
His disciples say unto him, Master, the Jews of late sought to stone thee; and goest thou thither again?
And when the tempter came to him, he said, If thou be the Son of God, command that these stones be made bread.
The Jews answered him, saying, For a good work we stone thee not; but for blasphemy; and because that thou, being a man, makest thyself God.
Then they reviled him, and said, Thou art his disciple; but we are Moses’ disciples.
And as he went out of the temple, one of his disciples saith unto him, Master, see what manner of stones and what buildings are here!
Now Moses in the law commanded us, that such should be stoned: but what sayest thou?
Jesus answered, Are there not twelve hours in the day? If any man walk in the day, he stumbleth not, because he seeth the light of this world.
But if a man walk in the night, he stumbleth, because there is no light in him.
Because that thou mayest understand, that there are yet but twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem for to worship.

the earth. This does not look consistent to me. If it took six days to form a small world like ours, we might certainly suppose that it would require more than one day to form the sun, which contains a quantity of matter sufficient to make some three hundred and fifty-four thousand worlds like this, and whose actual size or magnitude is fourteen hundred thousand times larger than our globe;…
And when the hour was come, he sat down, and the twelve apostles with him.
And in the evening he cometh with the twelve.
But if a man walk in the night, he stumbleth, because there is no light in him.
They change the night into day: the light is short because of darkness.
Jesus answered, Are there not twelve hours in the day? If any man walk in the day, he stumbleth not, because he seeth the light of this world.
Who leave the paths of uprightness, to walk in the ways of darkness;
Why is light given to a man whose way is hid, and whom God hath hedged in?
If I say, Surely the darkness shall cover me; even the night shall be light about me.
These things said he: and after that he saith unto them, Our friend Lazarus sleepeth; but I go, that I may awake him out of sleep.
Wherefore he saith, Awake thou that sleepest, and arise from the dead, and Christ shall give thee light.
And when he dies he shall not sleep, that is to say in the earth, but shall be changed in the twinkling of an eye, and shall be caught up, and his rest shall be glorious.
Then said his disciples, Lord, if he sleep, he shall do well.
For this we say unto you by the word of the Lord, that we which are alive and remain unto the coming of the Lord shall not prevent them which are asleep.
Then said Jesus unto them plainly, Lazarus is dead.
Then said his disciples, Lord, if he sleep, he shall do well.
Saying, Say ye, His disciples came by night, and stole him away while we slept.
And his disciples came to him, and awoke him, saying, Lord, save us: we perish.
And when he rose up from prayer, and was come to his disciples, he found them sleeping for sorrow,
Then the Lord awaked as one out of sleep, and like a mighty man that shouteth by reason of wine.
Then cometh he to his disciples, and saith unto them, Sleep on now, and take your rest: behold, the hour is at hand, and the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners.
Howbeit Jesus spake of his death: but they thought that he had spoken of taking of rest in sleep.
So they sat down with him upon the ground seven days and seven nights, and none spake a word unto him: for they saw that his grief was very great.
For if Jesus had given them rest, then would he not afterward have spoken of another day.
These things said he: and after that he saith unto them, Our friend Lazarus sleepeth; but I go, that I may awake him out of sleep.
Then said Jesus unto them plainly, Lazarus is dead.
¶ But the chief priests consulted that they might put Lazarus also to death;
The people therefore that was with him when he called Lazarus out of his grave, and raised him from the dead, bare record.
But Jesus said unto him, Follow me; and let the dead bury their dead.
Then they took away the stone from the place where the dead was laid. And Jesus lifted up his eyes, and said, Father, I thank thee that thou hast heard me.
Then said Jesus again unto them, I go my way, and ye shall seek me, and shall die in your sins: whither I go, ye cannot come.
And I am glad for your sakes that I was not there, to the intent ye may believe; nevertheless let us go unto him.
And he said unto him, If thy presence go not with me, carry us not up hence.
And he said unto him, Go in peace. So he departed from him a little way.
And he said unto him, I will not go; but I will depart to mine own land, and to my kindred.
And now I go unto the Father, because it is expedient that I should go unto the Father for your sakes.
And he said, Therefore said I unto you, that no man can come unto me, except it were given unto him of my Father.
Then said Thomas, which is called Didymus, unto his fellowdisciples, Let us also go, that we may die with him.
¶ But Thomas, one of the twelve, called Didymus, was not with them when Jesus came.
And Thomas answered and said unto him, My Lord and my God.
And it came to pass that he said unto the people: Gather together on the morrow, for I shall die; wherefore, I desire to speak unto the people before I shall die.
¶ And after eight days again his disciples were within, and Thomas with them: then came Jesus, the doors being shut, and stood in the midst, and said, Peace be unto you.
The nobleman saith unto him, Sir, come down ere my child die.
Then when Jesus came, he found that he had lain in the grave four days already.
And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures:
And when his disciples heard of it, they came and took up his corpse, and laid it in a tomb.
Jesus therefore again groaning in himself cometh to the grave. It was a cave, and a stone lay upon it.
And it came to pass that after two days and two nights they were about to take his body and lay it in a sepulchre, which they had made for the purpose of burying their dead.
And his disciples came, and took up the body, and buried it, and went and told Jesus.
Now Bethany was nigh unto Jerusalem, about fifteen furlongs off:
And when they came nigh to Jerusalem, unto Bethphage and Bethany, at the mount of Olives, he sendeth forth two of his disciples,
And Jesus entered into Jerusalem, and into the temple: and when he had looked round about upon all things, and now the eventide was come, he went out unto Bethany with the twelve.

very old olive trees mark tho spot, each of them supposed to have lived a thousand years. .... About two miles from Jerusalem, descending the rugged pathway, you come unexpectedly upon a village meetly concealed by rocks, and figs, and pomegranates— this is Bethany ! k the house of figs.* — the village of Martha, and her sister Mary. Several houses are inhabited by Arab fellah’s; built of good…
¶ And, behold, two of them went that same day to a village called Emmaus, which was from Jerusalem about threescore furlongs.
¶ And on the morrow, when they were come from Bethany, he was hungry:
And many of the Jews came to Martha and Mary, to comfort them concerning their brother.
Then many of the Jews which came to Mary, and had seen the things which Jesus did, believed on him.
The Jews then which were with her in the house, and comforted her, when they saw Mary, that she rose up hastily and went out, followed her, saying, She goeth unto the grave to weep there.
And they sent away Rebekah their sister, and her nurse, and Abraham’s servant, and his men.
And they put away the strange gods from among them, and served the Lord: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel.
Then Martha, as soon as she heard that Jesus was coming, went and met him: but Mary sat still in the house.
¶ Now it came to pass, as they went, that he entered into a certain village: and a certain woman named Martha received him into her house.
Then said Martha unto Jesus, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.
Then said Martha unto Jesus, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.
But Martha was cumbered about much serving, and came to him, and said, Lord, dost thou not care that my sister hath left me to serve alone? bid her therefore that she help me.
And the Lord said: What hast thou done? The voice of thy brother’s blood cries unto me from the ground.
But I know, that even now, whatsoever thou wilt ask of God, God will give it thee.
And it shall be given thee from the time thou shalt go, until the time thou shalt return, what thou shalt do.
Give to him that asketh thee, and from him that would borrow of thee turn not thou away.

And now, because thou hast done this with such unwearyingness, behold, I will bless thee forever; and I will make thee mighty in word and in deed, in faith and in works; yea, even that all things shall be done unto thee according to thy word, for thou shalt not ask that which is contrary to my will.
Now are we sure that thou knowest all things, and needest not that any man should ask thee: by this we believe that thou camest forth from God.
Give to him that asketh thee, and from him that would borrow of thee turn thou not away.
Jesus saith unto her, Thy brother shall rise again.
Jesus saith unto her, I that speak unto thee am he.
Jesus saith unto her, Go, call thy husband, and come hither.
Martha saith unto him, I know that he shall rise again in the resurrection at the last day.
She saith unto him, Yea, Lord: I believe that thou art the Christ, the Son of God, which should come into the world.
And she said, Behold, thy sister in law is gone back unto her people, and unto her gods: return thou after thy sister in law.
Martha saith unto him, I know that he shall rise again in the resurrection at the last day.
Jesus saith unto her, Thy brother shall rise again.
For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God: and the dead in Christ shall rise first:
And he hath risen again from the dead, that he might bring all men unto him, on conditions of repentance.
And go quickly, and tell his disciples that he is risen from the dead; and, behold, he goeth before you into Galilee; there shall ye see him: lo, I have told you.
Jesus said unto her, I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live:
And she said, Oh my lord, as thy soul liveth, my lord, I am the woman that stood by thee here, praying unto the Lord.
She said, No man, Lord. And Jesus said unto her, Neither do I condemn thee: go, and sin no more.
And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die. Believest thou this?
And whoso believeth not in me, and is not baptized, shall be damned.
I said therefore unto you, that ye shall die in your sins: for if ye believe not that I am he, ye shall die in your sins.
Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that believeth on me hath everlasting life.
And it shall come to pass that whosoever shall believe on the Son of God, the same shall have everlasting life.
She saith unto him, Yea, Lord: I believe that thou art the Christ, the Son of God, which should come into the world.
Jesus saith unto her, Said I not unto thee, that, if thou wouldest believe, thou shouldest see the glory of God?
And she shall bring forth a son, and thou shalt call his name Jesus: for he shall save his people from their sins.
And when she had so said, she went her way, and called Mary her sister secretly, saying, The Master is come, and calleth for thee.
And when Jesus saw her, he called her to him, and said unto her, Woman, thou art loosed from thine infirmity.
And Mary said, Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to thy word. And the angel departed from her.
Jesus saith unto her, Mary. She turned herself, and saith unto him, Rabboni; which is to say, Master.
And when she had thus said, she turned herself back, and saw Jesus standing, and knew not that it was Jesus.
As soon as she heard that, she arose quickly, and came unto him.

And when the woman saw that she was not hid, she came trembling, and falling down before him, she declared unto him before all the people for what cause she had touched him, and how she was healed immediately.
And he stood over her, and rebuked the fever; and it left her: and immediately she arose and ministered unto them.
And when she had brought them unto him to eat, he took hold of her, and said unto her, Come lie with me, my sister.
And the woman made haste, and ran, and shewed her husband, and said unto him, Behold, the man hath appeared unto me, that came unto me the other day.
Then came she and worshipped him, saying, Lord, help me.
Now Jesus was not yet come into the town, but was in that place where Martha met him.
And when he was gone out into the porch, another maid saw him, and said unto them that were there, This fellow was also with Jesus of Nazareth.
And, behold, the whole city came out to meet Jesus: and when they saw him, they besought him that he would depart out of their coasts.
The Jews then which were with her in the house, and comforted her, when they saw Mary, that she rose up hastily and went out, followed her, saying, She goeth unto the grave to weep there.
When Jesus therefore saw her weeping, and the Jews also weeping which came with her, he groaned in the spirit, and was troubled,
And when the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her, and said unto her, Weep not.
And many of the Jews came to Martha and Mary, to comfort them concerning their brother.
Then when Mary was come where Jesus was, and saw him, she fell down at his feet, saying unto him, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.
When Jesus therefore saw her weeping, and the Jews also weeping which came with her, he groaned in the spirit, and was troubled,
And she was in bitterness of soul, and prayed unto the Lord, and wept sore.
¶ But Mary stood without at the sepulchre weeping: and as she wept, she stooped down, and looked into the sepulchre,
And said, Where have ye laid him? They said unto him, Lord, come and see.
Then said they unto him, Where is he? He said, I know not.
And they said unto him, Where wilt thou that we prepare?
And the word of the Lord came unto him, saying,
And he saith unto them, Be not affrighted: Ye seek Jesus of Nazareth, which was crucified: he is risen; he is not here: behold the place where they laid him.
And they called unto Lot, and said unto him, Where are the men which came in to thee this night? bring them out unto us, that we may know them.
Jesus wept.
And when he had done this he wept again;
And cried with a loud voice, saying, Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb.
And Jesus cried with a loud voice, and gave up the ghost.
In my distress I cried unto the Lord, and he heard me.
This poor man cried, and the Lord heard him, and saved him out of all his troubles.
Then said the Jews, Behold how he loved him!
And said, Hail, King of the Jews! and they smote him with their hands.
And when he had said these words, the Jews departed, and had great reasoning among themselves.
And began to salute him, Hail, King of the Jews!
Then said the Jews, Will he kill himself? because he saith, Whither I go, ye cannot come.
The man departed, and told the Jews that it was Jesus, which had made him whole.
And some of them said, Could not this man, which opened the eyes of the blind, have caused that even this man should not have died?
Since the world began was it not heard that any man opened the eyes of one that was born blind.
And he took the blind man by the hand, and led him out of the town; and when he had spit on his eyes, and put his hands upon him, he asked him if he saw ought.
Then again called they the man that was blind, and said unto him, Give God the praise: we know that this man is a sinner.
Others said, These are not the words of him that hath a devil. Can a devil open the eyes of the blind?
And as Jesus passed by, he saw a man which was blind from his birth.
Jesus therefore again groaning in himself cometh to the grave. It was a cave, and a stone lay upon it.
Then when Jesus came, he found that he had lain in the grave four days already.

tions which it is generally admitted were uttered concerning Jesus Christ, fulfilled? Let us see. Jesus Christ was taken by wicked hands, hung upon the cross and crucified. He prayed for his enemies before he departed; he prayed that God would forgive them, because they knew not what they did, and then “bowed his head and gave up the ghost.” Where did the ghost or spirit of Christ go to after it…
Jesus said, Take ye away the stone. Martha, the sister of him that was dead, saith unto him, Lord, by this time he stinketh: for he hath been dead four days.
And the Lord said unto Moses, The man shall be surely put to death: all the congregation shall stone him with stones without the camp.

nodes.lb: GC 2018/04 — Elder Larry J. Echo Hawk
nodes.x: Even as Christ Forgives You, So Also Do Ye By Elder Larry J. Echo Hawk Of the Seventy We can all receive unspeakable peace and partnering with our Savior as we learn to freely forgive those who have trespassed against us. “Now upon the first day of the week, very early in the morning, they came unto the sepulchre, bringing the spices which they had prepared, and certain others with them. “And…

nodes.lb: GC 1974/04 — Elder Eldred G. Smith Patriarch to the Church Last spring my wife and I had the wonderful privilege of visiting the Holy Land. On the last day of our stay in Jerusalem, we left the hotel early in the morning and walked to the Garden Tomb. To our great delight, we were alone in the garden. A feeling of awesome reverence filled our hearts. We gazed at the hill Golgotha, the place
nodes.x: Three Days in the Tomb By Elder Eldred G. Smith Patriarch to the Church Last spring my wife and I had the wonderful privilege of visiting the Holy Land. On the last day of our stay in Jerusalem, we left the hotel early in the morning and walked to the Garden Tomb. To our great delight, we were alone in the garden. A feeling of awesome reverence filled our hearts. We gazed at the hill Golgotha,…
Jesus saith unto her, Said I not unto thee, that, if thou wouldest believe, thou shouldest see the glory of God?
And she said unto her, All that thou sayest unto me I will do.
For I, saith the Lord, will be unto her a wall of fire round about, and will be the glory in the midst of her.
Then they took away the stone from the place where the dead was laid. And Jesus lifted up his eyes, and said, Father, I thank thee that thou hast heard me.
Then took they up stones to cast at him: but Jesus hid himself, and went out of the temple, going through the midst of them, and so passed by.
Jesus said, Take ye away the stone. Martha, the sister of him that was dead, saith unto him, Lord, by this time he stinketh: for he hath been dead four days.
And I knew that thou hearest me always: but because of the people which stand by I said it, that they may believe that thou hast sent me.
For I have given unto them the words which thou gavest me; and they have received them, and have known surely that I came out from thee, and they have believed that thou didst send me.
Why askest thou me? ask them which heard me, what I have said unto them: behold, they know what I said.
And I said, Lord, they know that I imprisoned and beat in every synagogue them that believed on thee:
And I said: Yea, thou knowest that I believe all the words of my father.
Surely thou hast spoken in mine hearing, and I have heard the voice of thy words, saying,
And when he thus had spoken, he cried with a loud voice, Lazarus, come forth.
And it came to pass that he cried mightily unto the Lord all that day; and behold, the voice of the Lord came unto him, saying:
And he cried and said, Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame.
And when the unclean spirit had torn him, and cried with a loud voice, he came out of him.
And he that was dead came forth, bound hand and foot with graveclothes: and his face was bound about with a napkin. Jesus saith unto them, Loose him, and let him go.
Then took they the body of Jesus, and wound it in linen clothes with the spices, as the manner of the Jews is to bury.
And some began to spit on him, and to cover his face, and to buffet him, and to say unto him, Prophesy: and the servants did strike him with the palms of their hands.
And he, casting away his garment, rose, and came to Jesus.
Then many of the Jews which came to Mary, and had seen the things which Jesus did, believed on him.
For he mightily convinced the Jews, and that publickly, shewing by the scriptures that Jesus was Christ.
Because that by reason of him many of the Jews went away, and believed on Jesus.
And he was seen many days of them which came up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are his witnesses unto the people.
But some of them went their ways to the Pharisees, and told them what things Jesus had done.
And they which were sent were of the Pharisees.
And some of the Pharisees from among the multitude said unto him, Master, rebuke thy disciples.
Then came to Jesus scribes and Pharisees, which were of Jerusalem, saying,
And the Pharisees also, who were covetous, heard all these things: and they derided him.
And it came to pass that they did do all things even as Jesus had commanded them.
¶ Then gathered the chief priests and the Pharisees a council, and said, What do we? for this man doeth many miracles.
¶ Then the Pharisees went out, and held a council against him, how they might destroy him.

Therefore said some of the Pharisees, This man is not of God, because he keepeth not the sabbath day. Others said, How can a man that is a sinner do such miracles? And there was a division among them.
¶ The Pharisees heard that the people murmured such things concerning him; and the Pharisees and the chief priests sent officers to take him.
Saying, What shall we do to these men? for that indeed a notable miracle hath been done by them is manifest to all them that dwell in Jerusalem; and we cannot deny it.
If we let him thus alone, all men will believe on him: and the Romans shall come and take away both our place and nation.
If we believe not, yet he abideth faithful: he cannot deny himself.

So shall we come upon him in some place where he shall be found, and we will light upon him as the dew falleth on the ground: and of him and of all the men that are with him there shall not be left so much as one.
And this is his commandment, That we should believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ, and love one another, as he gave us commandment.
He saved others; himself he cannot save. If he be the King of Israel, let him now come down from the cross, and we will believe him.
For let not that man think that he shall receive any thing of the Lord.
And one of them, named Caiaphas, being the high priest that same year, said unto them, Ye know nothing at all,

_? Something is wanting here: perhaps a remark on the men- 
tion of Annas as the high-priest, whereas elsewhere Caiaphas 
appears to have been high-priest shortly before.
¶ And they that had laid hold on Jesus led him away to Caiaphas the high priest, where the scribes and the elders were assembled.
Then assembled together the chief priests, and the scribes, and the elders of the people, unto the palace of the high priest, who was called Caiaphas,
And led him away to Annas first; for he was father in law to Caiaphas, which was the high priest that same year.
Now when the high priest and the captain of the temple and the chief priests heard these things, they doubted of them whereunto this would grow.
Nor consider that it is expedient for us, that one man should die for the people, and that the whole nation perish not.
Now behold, it was not expedient that man should be reclaimed from this temporal death, for that would destroy the great plan of happiness.
For scarcely for a righteous man will one die: yet peradventure for a good man some would even dare to die.
That being justified by his grace, we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life.
And this spake he not of himself: but being high priest that year, he prophesied that Jesus should die for that nation;
And when he had gathered all the chief priests and scribes of the people together, he demanded of them where Christ should be born.

But all that heard him were amazed, and said; Is not this he that destroyed them which called on this name in Jerusalem, and came hither for that intent, that he might bring them bound unto the chief priests?
And he spake boldly in the name of the Lord Jesus, and disputed against the Grecians: but they went about to slay him.
And it came to pass that his high priest murdered him as he sat upon his throne.
And that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name among all nations, beginning at Jerusalem.
And not for that nation only, but that also he should gather together in one the children of God that were scattered abroad.

Yea, he sent a proclamation among all the people, that they should gather together their women, and their children, their flocks and their herds, and all their substance, save it were their land, unto one place.
And before him shall be gathered all nations: and he shall separate them one from another, as a shepherd divideth his sheep from the goats:
And he caused that a multitude should be gathered together that they might witness what the Lord had done for his son, and also for those that were with him.
And in those times there was no peace to him that went out, nor to him that came in, but great vexations were upon all the inhabitants of the countries.
Then from that day forth they took counsel together for to put him to death.
¶ When they heard that, they were cut to the heart, and took counsel to slay them.
But he forsook the counsel which the old men gave him, and took counsel with the young men that were brought up with him, that stood before him.
¶ And after that many days were fulfilled, the Jews took counsel to kill him:
And after three days, having counseled with his priests, he caused that he should again be brought before him.
¶ Then went the Pharisees, and took counsel how they might entangle him in his talk.
Jesus therefore walked no more openly among the Jews; but went thence unto a country near to the wilderness, into a city called Ephraim, and there continued with his disciples.
After these things Jesus walked in Galilee: for he would not walk in Jewry, because the Jews sought to kill him.

But he went out, and began to publish it much, and to blaze abroad the matter, insomuch that Jesus could no more openly enter into the city, but was without in desert places: and they came to him from every quarter.
¶ From that time many of his disciples went back, and walked no more with him.
And he came to Ephesus, and left them there: but he himself entered into the synagogue, and reasoned with the Jews.
¶ And the Jews’ passover was nigh at hand: and many went out of the country up to Jerusalem before the passover, to purify themselves.
And the passover, a feast of the Jews, was nigh.
¶ And the Jews’ passover was at hand, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem,
Now the feast of unleavened bread drew nigh, which is called the Passover.
And the children of Israel that were present kept the passover at that time, and the feast of unleavened bread seven days.
And they went, and found as he had said unto them: and they made ready the passover.
Then sought they for Jesus, and spake among themselves, as they stood in the temple, What think ye, that he will not come to the feast?
Then the Jews sought him at the feast, and said, Where is he?
(For of necessity he must release one unto them at the feast.)
Now at that feast he released unto them one prisoner, whomsoever they desired.
These words spake Jesus in the treasury, as he taught in the temple: and no man laid hands on him; for his hour was not yet come.
Now both the chief priests and the Pharisees had given a commandment, that, if any man knew where he were, he should shew it, that they might take him.
¶ Then came the officers to the chief priests and Pharisees; and they said unto them, Why have ye not brought him?
And he charged them, saying, Take heed, beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, and of the leaven of Herod.
¶ And they send unto him certain of the Pharisees and of the Herodians, to catch him in his words.
